title: Standard Error — The Universal Reference
text:
By the end of this lesson, students should be able to:

Explain in plain English what standard error measures
Identify which SE formula applies in each inferential context
Recognize that CI and test statistic formulas share the same SE structure
Compute SE for means, proportions, and regression coefficients




Standard error (SE) is the standard deviation of a sampling distribution — it measures how uncertain your estimate is. Every confidence interval and test statistic divides by one.
The structure is always the same regardless of what you’re estimating:
\[\text{CI}: \quad \hat{\theta} \pm (\text{critical value}) \times SE(\hat{\theta})\]
\[\text{Test statistic}: \quad \frac{\hat{\theta} - \theta_0}{SE(\hat{\theta})}\]
Larger SE → wider CI → harder to detect a difference.













Situation
SE Formula
Distribution




One-sample, σ known
\(\sigma / \sqrt{n}\)
Z


One-sample, σ unknown
\(s / \sqrt{n}\)
t, df = n−1


Two-sample pooled
\(s_p\sqrt{1/n_1 + 1/n_2}\)
t, df = n₁+n₂−2


Two-sample Welch
\(\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}\)
t, df = ν (Welch)


Paired
\(s_d / \sqrt{n}\)
t, df = n−1



\[s_p = \sqrt{\frac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}}\]






Situation
SE Formula




Hypothesis test (use null p₀)
\(\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}\)


Confidence interval (use p̂)
\(\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}\)









Parameter
SE Formula




SLR slope β̂₁
\(\sqrt{MSE / S_{xx}}\)


MLR coefficient β̂ⱼ
\(\sqrt{MSE \cdot C_{jj}}\)


CI on mean response
\(\sqrt{MSE[1/n + (x_0-\bar{x})^2/S_{xx}]}\)


PI on future obs
adds +1 under the square root




R gives se(β̂ⱼ) in the Std. Error column of summary(fit).







Code
# Simulate a sample and compute SE
set.seed(42)
x <- rnorm(30, mean = 50, sd = 10)

n    <- length(x)
xbar <- mean(x)
s    <- sd(x)
se   <- s / sqrt(n)

cat("n =", n, "\n")


n = 30 


Code
cat("x̄ =", round(xbar, 3), "\n")


x̄ = 50.686 


Code
cat("s =", round(s, 3), "\n")


s = 12.55 


Code
cat("SE = s/√n =", round(se, 3), "\n")


SE = s/√n = 2.291 


Code
cat("95% CI: (", round(xbar - 1.96*se, 2), ",", round(xbar + 1.96*se, 2), ")\n")


95% CI: ( 46.19 , 55.18 )





All SEs follow the same logic:

Start with the variance of the estimate (how variable is θ̂?)
Take the square root → standard deviation of the estimate = SE
n appears in the denominator → more data = smaller SE = narrower CI

For regression, MSE plays the role of σ² (variance estimated from residuals), and the extra terms account for the position of x₀ relative to the data centroid.



Modify the code above:

Change n from 30 to 100. How does SE change?
Change sd from 10 to 20. How does SE change?
What happens to the 95% CI as n → ∞?





Standard Error (master table)
Degrees of Freedom
Confidence Intervals

title: Confidence Intervals
text:
State the correct interpretation of a confidence interval
Choose Z vs t based on what is known
Construct CI on μ (Z and t), σ², and p
Determine sample size to achieve a target margin of error




A confidence interval gives a range of plausible values for an unknown population parameter. “95% confident” means: if we repeated this process many times, 95% of the intervals we build would contain the true parameter.

Teacher’s phrasing: “We are [100(1−α)]% confident that [parameter in context] is between [lower] and [upper].”

It does NOT mean there is a 95% chance μ is in this particular interval.



\[\text{Estimate} \pm \text{Critical value} \times \text{Standard Error}\]






Condition
Use




σ known
Z


σ unknown, normal population
t (df = n−1)


σ unknown, n ≥ 40
Z (CLT approximation)







Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



\[\frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}} \leq \sigma^2 \leq \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-1}}\]

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



\[n = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma}{E}\right)^2\]

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.




Using Z when σ is unknown and n is small
Forgetting to take square root for CI on σ (vs σ²)
Using α instead of α/2 for two-sided intervals
Misinterpreting the CI as P(μ ∈ CI) = 0.95




Run the interactive blocks above and verify these results:

Confidence level → width: Change conf_level to 0.99 in the CI on μ block. How does the width change? Why?
Sample size → width: Change n from 20 to 80. By what factor does the width shrink? (Hint: SE = s/√n.)
χ² asymmetry: In the CI on σ² block, note that the bounds are not symmetric around s². Why not? (Hint: the χ² distribution is skewed.)
Sample size tradeoff: In the sample size block, halve the margin of error E from 5 to 2.5. How does required n change?

title: Factorial ANOVA
text:
Distinguish main effects from interaction effects
Construct the ANOVA table for a two-factor factorial design
Apply the 3× SD rule for constant variance check
Interpret an interaction plot and apply the marginality principle




A factorial design tests multiple factors simultaneously and — critically — can detect interactions: situations where the effect of one factor depends on the level of another.
Interaction present: the optimal level of A depends on which level of B you’re at. Interaction absent: the optimal level of A is the same regardless of B.






Source
df
MS
F₀




A
a−1
MS_A
MS_A/MS_E


B
b−1
MS_B
MS_B/MS_E


AB (interaction)
(a−1)(b−1)
MS_AB
MS_AB/MS_E


Error
ab(n−1)
MS_E



Total
abn−1









trimws() is mandatory after summary(fit)[[1]] — R pads row names with trailing spaces
Hierarchical principle — if AB is in the model, both A and B stay even if one is non-significant
3× SD rule — if one cell’s within-cell SD is ≈3× larger than others, constant variance is not reasonable






Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, agricolae, tidyverse)

# Viscosity experiment: 2 pH levels × 2 catalyst types, n=4 per cell
set.seed(33)
df <- expand.grid(pH = factor(c("Low","High")),
                  catalyst = factor(c("A","B")),
                  rep = 1:4) |>
  mutate(viscosity = c(rnorm(4, 82, 3), rnorm(4, 91, 3),
                       rnorm(4, 79, 3), rnorm(4, 76, 3))) |>
  select(-rep)

fit <- aov(viscosity ~ pH * catalyst, data = df)

aov_tbl <- summary(fit)[[1]]
rownames(aov_tbl) <- trimws(rownames(aov_tbl))
print(aov_tbl)


            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
pH           1   0.93   0.927  0.0258 0.8751
catalyst     1   1.74   1.742  0.0485 0.8295
pH:catalyst  1   0.02   0.019  0.0005 0.9818
Residuals   12 431.37  35.948               




Code
df |>
  group_by(pH, catalyst) |>
  summarise(mean_visc = mean(viscosity), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = pH, y = mean_visc, color = catalyst, group = catalyst)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Interaction Plot: Viscosity",
       y = "Mean Viscosity", x = "pH") +
  theme_bw()





Interaction plot: if lines are parallel, no interaction






Code
# 3× SD rule: within-cell SDs
df |>
  group_by(pH, catalyst) |>
  summarise(sd = sd(viscosity), .groups = "drop") |>
  print()


# A tibble: 4 × 3
  pH    catalyst    sd
  <fct> <fct>    <dbl>
1 High  A         7.69
2 High  B         7.62
3 Low   A         1.53
4 Low   B         4.94


Code
# LSD on marginal means of significant factor
MSE  <- aov_tbl["Residuals", "Mean Sq"]
df_E <- aov_tbl["Residuals", "Df"]
a    <- nlevels(df$pH); b <- nlevels(df$catalyst); n <- 4
n_i  <- b * n   # observations per level of pH (marginal mean)

LSD_pH <- qt(0.975, df_E) * sqrt(MSE * 2 / n_i)
cat("LSD for pH marginal means:", round(LSD_pH, 3), "\n")


LSD for pH marginal means: 6.532 






Is the interaction significant? How do you interpret the interaction plot?
Apply the 3× SD rule — is constant variance reasonable?
If the interaction is not significant, which factor should drive recommendations?





Factorial ANOVA

a    <- nlevels(df$pH); b <- nlevels(df$catalyst); n <- 4
n_i  <- b * n   # observations per level of pH (marginal mean)

LSD_pH <- qt(0.975, df_E) * sqrt(MSE * 2 / n_i)
cat("LSD for pH marginal means:", round(LSD_pH, 3), "\n")


LSD for pH marginal means: 6.532

title: Drill: Simulate the CLT
text:
Build intuition for the sampling distribution of x̄ and the Central Limit Theorem by simulation. This underpins every CI and test statistic in the course.



Use this drill when you need to:

Explain why we can use Z/t even when the population is not normal
Demonstrate that SE = σ/√n shrinks with larger n
Show that the sampling distribution of x̄ is approximately normal for large n






Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, tidyverse)






Define a non-normal population
Draw many samples of size n, compute x̄ for each
Plot the sampling distribution and overlay the theoretical normal
Compute SE and verify it matches sd(x̄ values)






Code
set.seed(42)

# Non-normal population: exponential with mean = 20
pop_rate <- 1/20
pop_mean <- 20
pop_sd   <- 20   # exponential: SD = mean

# Parameters
n_sim  <- 10000  # number of samples to draw
n_samp <- 30     # sample size

# Simulate
xbars <- replicate(n_sim, mean(rexp(n_samp, rate = pop_rate)))

# Theoretical SE
se_theory <- pop_sd / sqrt(n_samp)

cat("Theoretical SE = σ/√n =", round(se_theory, 4), "\n")


Theoretical SE = σ/√n = 3.6515 


Code
cat("Simulated  SE = sd(x̄) =", round(sd(xbars), 4), "\n")


Simulated  SE = sd(x̄) = 3.648 


Code
# Plot
tibble(xbar = xbars) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = xbar)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 60,
                 fill = "steelblue", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, args = list(mean = pop_mean, sd = se_theory),
                color = "firebrick", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = sprintf("Sampling dist of x̄: Exponential pop, n = %d", n_samp),
       x = "Sample mean", y = "Density") +
  theme_bw()





Sampling distribution of x̄: exponential population, n=30







Change n_samp_new below and click Run Code to see how the sampling distribution shape changes with sample size.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.




At what sample size does the sampling distribution look approximately normal for an exponential population?
When you double n, by what factor does SE decrease?
Which part of the CLT result becomes the denominator in a Z-test or t-test?

title: Drill: Fill an ANOVA Table
text:
Build fluency with reconstructing complete ANOVA tables from partial information — a common exam task for both regression and DOE contexts.



Use this drill when you see: a partial ANOVA table with some cells blank, and you need to fill in SS, df, MS, F₀, σ̂, and R².



Regression ANOVA reconstruction:

Identify which cells are given (SS, df, MS, F₀, R², n, k)
Compute missing SS from SS_T = SS_R + SS_E
Compute missing df from df_T = n−1, df_R = k, df_E = n−k−1
Compute MS = SS/df
Compute F₀ = MS_R/MS_E
Compute σ̂ = √MS_E, R² = SS_R/SS_T

DOE ANOVA reconstruction:

Compute MS_E from F₀ = MS_Factor/MS_E → MS_E = MS_Factor/F₀
Compute SS_E = MS_E × df_E
Compute remaining SS by addition




Enter the given values and click Run Code to fill the table.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.




Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.




In Scenario B: if σ̂² had been 12 instead of 6.5 (same F₀), what would change?
In the DOE example: is the treatment effect significant? How close is F₀ to F_crit?
What formula connects MS_E to σ̂?





ANOVA Table Examples
SS Decomposition

title: Drill: Build a Control Chart
text:
Build fluency with the qcc control chart workflow: R chart → X̄ chart → identify out-of-control → revise limits → process capability.



Use this drill when you have subgroup measurement data and need to determine whether a process is in statistical control and whether it meets specification limits.




Load data (subgroup matrix: rows = subgroups, cols = observations)
Build R chart first — estimates σ̂
Build X̄ chart using σ̂ from R chart
Identify out-of-control subgroups ($violations)
Remove and revise limits
Compute Cp and Cpk against spec limits
State conclusion in teacher’s phrasing






Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, qcc)







Code
data("pistonrings")
diameter <- qcc.groups(pistonrings$diameter, pistonrings$sample)

r_chart <- qcc(diameter, type = "R", nsigmas = 3,
               title = "R Chart — Piston Ring Diameter")





R Chart — check for out-of-control range




Code
cat("R̄  (center):", round(r_chart$center, 6), "\n")


R̄  (center): 0.023425 


Code
cat("UCL_R:", round(r_chart$limits[2], 6), "\n")


UCL_R: 0.049531 


Code
cat("R chart violations:", r_chart$violations$beyond.limits, "\n")


R chart violations:  




Code
xbar_chart <- qcc(diameter, type = "xbar", nsigmas = 3,
                  std.dev = sd.R(diameter),
                  title = "X̄ Chart — Piston Ring Diameter")





X̄ Chart — check for out-of-control means




Code
cat("x̄̄  (center):", round(xbar_chart$center, 6), "\n")


x̄̄  (center): 74.0036 


Code
cat("UCL:", round(xbar_chart$limits[2], 6), "\n")


UCL: 74.01712 


Code
cat("LCL:", round(xbar_chart$limits[1], 6), "\n")


LCL: 73.99009 


Code
cat("σ̂ :", round(xbar_chart$std.dev, 6), "\n")


σ̂ : 0.010071 


Code
cat("X̄ chart violations:", xbar_chart$violations$beyond.limits, "\n")


X̄ chart violations: 38 39 




Code
# Spec limits: LSL = 73.95, USL = 74.05
pc <- process.capability(xbar_chart, spec.limits = c(73.95, 74.05))





Process capability





Process Capability Analysis

Call:
process.capability(object = xbar_chart, spec.limits = c(73.95,     74.05))

Number of obs = 200          Target = 74
       Center = 74              LSL = 73.95
       StdDev = 0.01007         USL = 74.05

Capability indices:

      Value   2.5%  97.5%
Cp    1.655  1.492  1.817
Cp_l  1.774  1.623  1.926
Cp_u  1.536  1.403  1.668
Cp_k  1.536  1.378  1.693
Cpm   1.558  1.397  1.719

Exp<LSL 0%   Obs<LSL 0%
Exp>USL 0%   Obs>USL 0%


Code
print(pc, digits = 6)

[1] 200

$center
[1] 74.0036

$std.dev
[1] 0.0100709

$target
[1] 74

$spec.limits
  LSL   USL 
73.95 74.05

Is Cpk ≥ 1.33? What does that mean for the process?

This is a living statistical methods resource for INEG 2314H at [your institution].


This resource is built on two frameworks:
CPS Knowledge Structure — every page is classified as a Construct (foundational quantity), Procedure (inferential technique), or Script (fixed-sequence workflow). See knowledge-dimensions for the theoretical grounding.
LLM-Maintained Wiki — the wiki is written and maintained incrementally by an AI agent from lecture slides, homework files, and R projects. See llm-wiki for the architecture.

The maintenance folder tracks the agent queue, change log, and link audits

title: Drill: Factorial ANOVA in R
text:
Build fluency with the two-factor and 2^k ANOVA workflow in R: import wide data → reshape long → fit model → trimws() → extract values → interaction plot → LSD.



Use this drill when you have a factorial experiment with 2+ factors and need to test main effects, interactions, and do pairwise comparisons.




Import (wide format: one column per replicate)
pivot_longer() — reshape to long format
aov() — fit factorial model
summary()[[1]] + trimws() — extract ANOVA table
Check interaction significance
If interaction non-significant: LSD.test() on significant main effect marginal means
Interaction plot — interpret parallel vs crossing lines






Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, agricolae, tidyverse)







Code
# Warping of copper plates: factor A = temperature (4 levels), B = Cu content (3 levels)
# Entered in wide format (R1, R2 = replicates)
df_wide <- tribble(
  ~temp, ~cu,  ~R1,  ~R2,
  "50",  "40",  17,   20,
  "50",  "60",  12,    9,
  "50",  "80",  16,   12,
  "50", "100",  21,   17,
  "75",  "40",  12,   13,
  "75",  "60",  18,   21,
  "75",  "80",  10,   12,
  "75", "100",  24,   21,
  "100", "40",  14,   15,
  "100", "60",  18,   20,
  "100", "80",  14,   11,
  "100","100",  23,   22,
  "125", "40",  19,   17,
  "125", "60",  23,   20,
  "125", "80",  19,   16,
  "125","100",  24,   28
)

# Reshape to long
df <- df_wide |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(R1, R2), names_to = "rep", values_to = "warping") |>
  mutate(temp = factor(temp), cu = factor(cu))

head(df)


# A tibble: 6 × 4
  temp  cu    rep   warping
  <fct> <fct> <chr>   <dbl>
1 50    40    R1         17
2 50    40    R2         20
3 50    60    R1         12
4 50    60    R2          9
5 50    80    R1         16
6 50    80    R2         12




Code
fit <- aov(warping ~ temp * cu, data = df)

aov_tbl <- summary(fit)[[1]]
rownames(aov_tbl) <- trimws(rownames(aov_tbl))  # MANDATORY
print(aov_tbl)


          Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
temp       3 127.62  42.542 10.8042 0.0003992 ***
cu         3 333.63 111.208 28.2434 1.254e-06 ***
temp:cu    9 159.63  17.736  4.5044 0.0043805 ** 
Residuals 16  63.00   3.937                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1




Code
MSE  <- aov_tbl["Residuals", "Mean Sq"]
df_E <- aov_tbl["Residuals", "Df"]
N    <- nrow(df); a <- nlevels(df$temp); b <- nlevels(df$cu); n <- 2

cat("MSE =", round(MSE, 4), "\n")


MSE = 3.9375 


Code
cat("σ̂  =", round(sqrt(MSE), 4), "\n")


σ̂  = 1.9843 


Code
# Is interaction significant?
p_int <- aov_tbl["temp:cu", "Pr(>F)"]
cat("Interaction p-value:", round(p_int, 4), "\n")


Interaction p-value: 0.0044 




Code
df |>
  group_by(temp, cu) |>
  summarise(mean_w = mean(warping), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = cu, y = mean_w, color = temp, group = temp)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  labs(title = "Warping: Interaction Plot",
       x = "Cu Content (%)", y = "Mean Warping", color = "Temp (°C)") +
  theme_bw()





Interaction plot: parallel lines → no interaction






Code
# If interaction not significant, apply LSD on significant main effect
# LSD for Cu (b=4 levels, n_i = a*n observations per Cu level)
n_i_cu <- a * n
LSD_cu  <- qt(0.975, df_E) * sqrt(MSE * 2 / n_i_cu)
cat("\nLSD for Cu marginal means:", round(LSD_cu, 4), "\n")



LSD for Cu marginal means: 2.1033 


Code
# Marginal means for Cu
tapply(df$warping, df$cu, mean) |> round(3) |> print()


   100     40     60     80 
22.500 15.875 17.625 13.750 





Modify factor levels, swap the interaction term, or try your own response values. Click Run Code to fit.

title: Drill: Factorial ANOVA in R
text: Build fluency with the two-factor and 2^k ANOVA workflow in R: import wide data → reshape long → fit model → trimws() → extract values → interaction plot → LSD.

title: Drill: Factorial ANOVA in R
text: Use this drill when you have a factorial experiment with 2+ factors and need to test main effects, interactions, and do pairwise comparisons.

title: Drill: Factorial ANOVA in R
text:
Import (wide format: one column per replicate)
pivot_longer() — reshape to long format
aov() — fit factorial model
summary()[[1]] + trimws() — extract ANOVA table
Check interaction significance
If interaction non-significant: LSD.test() on significant main effect marginal means
Interaction plot — interpret parallel vs crossing lines

title: Drill: Factorial ANOVA in R
text:
Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, agricolae, tidyverse)

title: Drill: Factorial ANOVA in R
text:
Code
# Warping of copper plates: factor A = temperature (4 levels), B = Cu content (3 levels)
# Entered in wide format (R1, R2 = replicates)
df_wide <- tribble(
  ~temp, ~cu,  ~R1,  ~R2,
  "50",  "40",  17,   20,
  "50",  "60",  12,    9,
  "50",  "80",  16,   12,
  "50", "100",  21,   17,
  "75",  "40",  12,   13,
  "75",  "60",  18,   21,
  "75",  "80",  10,   12,
  "75", "100",  24,   21,
  "100", "40",  14,   15,
  "100", "60",  18,   20,
  "100", "80",  14,   11,
  "100","100",  23,   22,
  "125", "40",  19,   17,
  "125", "60",  23,   20,
  "125", "80",  19,   16,
  "125","100",  24,   28
)

# Reshape to long
df <- df_wide |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(R1, R2), names_to = "rep", values_to = "warping") |>
  mutate(temp = factor(temp), cu = factor(cu))

head(df)


# A tibble: 6 × 4
  temp  cu    rep   warping
  <fct> <fct> <chr>   <dbl>
1 50    40    R1         17
2 50    40    R2         20
3 50    60    R1         12
4 50    60    R2          9
5 50    80    R1         16
6 50    80    R2         12




Code
fit <- aov(warping ~ temp * cu, data = df)

aov_tbl <- summary(fit)[[1]]
rownames(aov_tbl) <- trimws(rownames(aov_tbl))  # MANDATORY
print(aov_tbl)


          Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
temp       3 127.62  42.542 10.8042 0.0003992 ***
cu         3 333.63 111.208 28.2434 1.254e-06 ***
temp:cu    9 159.63  17.736  4.5044 0.0043805 ** 
Residuals 16  63.00   3.937                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1




Code
MSE  <- aov_tbl["Residuals", "Mean Sq"]
df_E <- aov_tbl["Residuals", "Df"]
N    <- nrow(df); a <- nlevels(df$temp); b <- nlevels(df$cu); n <- 2

cat("MSE =", round(MSE, 4), "\n")


MSE = 3.9375 


Code
cat("σ̂  =", round(sqrt(MSE), 4), "\n")


σ̂  = 1.9843 


Code
# Is interaction significant?
p_int <- aov_tbl["temp:cu", "Pr(>F)"]
cat("Interaction p-value:", round(p_int, 4), "\n")


Interaction p-value: 0.0044 




Code
df |>
  group_by(temp, cu) |>
  summarise(mean_w = mean(warping), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = cu, y = mean_w, color = temp, group = temp)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  labs(title = "Warping: Interaction Plot",
       x = "Cu Content (%)", y = "Mean Warping", color = "Temp (°C)") +
  theme_bw()





Interaction plot: parallel lines → no interaction






Code
# If interaction not significant, apply LSD on significant main effect
# LSD for Cu (b=4 levels, n_i = a*n observations per Cu level)
n_i_cu <- a * n
LSD_cu  <- qt(0.975, df_E) * sqrt(MSE * 2 / n_i_cu)
cat("\nLSD for Cu marginal means:", round(LSD_cu, 4), "\n")



LSD for Cu marginal means: 2.1033 


Code
# Marginal means for Cu
tapply(df$warping, df$cu, mean) |> round(3) |> print()


   100     40     60     80 
22.500 15.875 17.625 13.750

title: Drill: Fit and Interpret a Regression Model
text:
Build fluency with the complete regression workflow: fit → ANOVA → summary → confint → predict → diagnostics. This sequence appears in full on exams.



Use this drill when you see: a dataset with one continuous response and one or more predictors, and you need to fit a regression model, test coefficients, build intervals, and check assumptions.




Import and inspect data
lm() — fit the model
anova() — ANOVA table (F-test, SS decomposition)
summary() — coefficient table, R², σ̂
confint() — CI on βⱼ (watch column names!)
predict() — CI on mean response and PI on future observation
Residual diagnostics — check assumptions






Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, tidyverse)







Code
# Wire bond pull strength data (Montgomery Table 12.1)
df <- tribble(
  ~strength, ~length, ~height,
   9.95, 2,  50,  24.45, 8, 110,  31.75, 11, 120,  35.00, 10, 550,
  25.02, 8, 295,  16.86, 4, 200,  14.38,  2, 375,   9.60,  2,  52,
  24.35, 9, 100,  27.50, 8, 300,  17.08,  4, 412,  37.00, 11, 400,
  41.95,12, 500,  11.66, 2, 360,  21.65,  4, 205,  17.89,  4, 400,
  69.00,20, 600,  10.30, 1, 585,  34.93, 10, 540,  46.59, 15, 250
)

glimpse(df)


Rows: 20
Columns: 3
$ strength <dbl> 9.95, 24.45, 31.75, 35.00, 25.02, 16.86, 14.38, 9.60, 24.35, …
$ length   <dbl> 2, 8, 11, 10, 8, 4, 2, 2, 9, 8, 4, 11, 12, 2, 4, 4, 20, 1, 10…
$ height   <dbl> 50, 110, 120, 550, 295, 200, 375, 52, 100, 300, 412, 400, 500…




Code
fit <- lm(strength ~ length + height, data = df)




Code
anova(fit)


Analysis of Variance Table

Response: strength
          Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
length     1 4018.0  4018.0 663.019 4.652e-15 ***
height     1   88.6    88.6  14.619   0.00136 ** 
Residuals 17  103.0     6.1                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


Code
# Extract SSR, SSE, MSE manually
a   <- anova(fit)
SSR <- sum(a[1:(nrow(a)-1), "Sum Sq"])
SSE <- a["Residuals", "Sum Sq"]
MSE <- a["Residuals", "Mean Sq"]
cat("SSR =", round(SSR, 3), "  SSE =", round(SSE, 3),
    "  MSE =", round(MSE, 3), "\n")


SSR = 4106.596   SSE = 103.023   MSE = 6.06 




Code
summary(fit)



Call:
lm(formula = strength ~ length + height, data = df)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-3.7857 -1.4659 -0.3836  1.2563  5.9610 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 2.122485   1.272092   1.669  0.11353    
length      2.751591   0.116739  23.570 2.01e-14 ***
height      0.012489   0.003266   3.824  0.00136 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 2.462 on 17 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9755,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9726 
F-statistic: 338.8 on 2 and 17 DF,  p-value: 2.013e-14


Code
# Key scalars
cat("\nR²     =", round(summary(fit)$r.squared, 4), "\n")



R²     = 0.9755 


Code
cat("R²_adj =", round(summary(fit)$adj.r.squared, 4), "\n")


R²_adj = 0.9726 


Code
cat("σ̂     =", round(summary(fit)$sigma, 4), "\n")


σ̂     = 2.4617 




Code
# 95% CI on each coefficient
ci <- confint(fit, level = 0.95)
print(ci)


                   2.5 %     97.5 %
(Intercept) -0.561393612 4.80636353
length       2.505293337 2.99788950
height       0.005597386 0.01937962


Code
# Extraction by name (critical: use exact column name)
cat("\nβ̂₁ (length) CI lower:", round(ci["length", "2.5 %"], 4), "\n")



β̂₁ (length) CI lower: 2.5053 


Code
cat("β̂₁ (length) CI upper:", round(ci["length", "97.5 %"], 4), "\n")


β̂₁ (length) CI upper: 2.9979 




Code
# Predict at length = 10, height = 300
x0 <- data.frame(length = 10, height = 300)

ci_mean <- predict(fit, newdata = x0, interval = "confidence", level = 0.95)
pi_obs  <- predict(fit, newdata = x0, interval = "prediction",  level = 0.95)

cat("Point estimate:", round(ci_mean[,"fit"], 3), "\n")


Point estimate: 33.385 


Code
cat("95% CI on mean: (", round(ci_mean[,"lwr"], 3), ",",
                        round(ci_mean[,"upr"], 3), ")\n")


95% CI on mean: ( 32.026 , 34.744 )


Code
cat("95% PI on obs:  (", round(pi_obs[,"lwr"], 3), ",",
                        round(pi_obs[,"upr"], 3), ")\n")


95% PI on obs:  ( 28.016 , 38.754 )



Code
df <- df |>
  mutate(resid = residuals(fit), fitted_val = fitted(fit))

ggplot(df, aes(x = fitted_val, y = resid)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(x = "Fitted", y = "Residuals") + theme_bw()

ggplot(df, aes(sample = resid)) +
  stat_qq(color = "steelblue") + stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Normal Q-Q") + theme_bw()







Residuals vs Fitted







Normal Q-Q






Residual diagnostics






Modify the prediction point or fit a simple model on your own data. Click Run Code to execute.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.




What does β̂₁ = [value] mean in words?
Is the 95% CI on β̂₁ entirely positive/negative? What does that tell you?
Why is the PI always wider than the CI at the same x₀?

title: Knowledge Graph Navigation
text:
This resource supports both linear study and graph-based exploration.
The linear path acts like a road. The wiki acts like a map.



Every wiki page links to the pages it depends on and the pages that use it. Following these links reveals the conceptual structure of statistics as a connected graph — not a list of isolated topics.
Example: Standard Error
standard-error connects to:

confidence-intervals — CI formula uses SE
hypothesis-testing-overview — test statistic = (estimate − null) / SE
regression-slr — β̂₁ and β̂₀ have their own SEs from MSE
control-charts — σ̂ from R̄/d₂ or s̄/c₄ plays the SE role

Once you understand SE as “standard deviation of the sampling distribution,” every procedure that uses it becomes a variant of the same idea.




These are the constructs most worth deeply understanding first — once they click, multiple procedures unlock simultaneously.



Concept
Why it’s a threshold
Unlocks




Standard Error
Every CI and test statistic divides by one
CI, HT, regression, control charts


SS Decomposition
SS_T = SS_Model + SS_Error underlies every ANOVA table
Regression, CRD, RCBD, factorial, 2^k


Sampling Distributions
The link between a single sample and the population
Everything in inference


Degrees of Freedom
Which df goes with which test
Every t, χ², and F test















Idea
Where it first appears
Where it recurs




SE = σ/√n
Unit 1 CI
Regression βⱼ SE, control chart σ̂


SS decomposition
Regression ANOVA
CRD, RCBD, factorial ANOVA


F-test
Regression F-test
One-way ANOVA, factorial ANOVA, 2^k


Residual diagnostics
Regression model adequacy
CRD and RCBD model checking


Variance estimation
σ² → MSE in regression
→ MS_Error in ANOVA







If you’re confused about p-values: hypothesis-testing-overview → which-test → reading-r-output
If you’re confused about ANOVA tables: ss-decomposition → regression-ht → crd-one-way-anova → anova-table-examples
If you’re confused about which chart to use: control-charts → which-test-flowchart
If you’re confused about 2^k designs: ss-decomposition → factorial-anova → 2k-factorial-design → 2k-factorial-examples

title: CRD and One-Way ANOVA
text:
Identify when a CRD is appropriate and when blocking is needed
Set up the one-way ANOVA model and construct the ANOVA table
Apply Fisher’s LSD for pairwise comparisons
Run both balanced and unbalanced CRD in R and extract key values




A CRD randomly assigns experimental units to treatment levels. Use CRD when experimental units are homogeneous. If there is a known nuisance variable (time, batch, operator), use RCBD instead — blocking removes that variation from SS_Error.






Source
SS
df
MS
F₀




Treatments
SS_Trt
a−1
MS_Trt
MS_Trt/MS_E


Error
SS_E
N−a
MS_E



Total
SS_T
N−1





\[\hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{MS_E}, \quad \text{Fisher LSD} = t_{\alpha/2,\,N-a}\sqrt{MS_E\left(\tfrac{1}{n_i}+\tfrac{1}{n_j}\right)}\]





Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, rio, tidyverse)

# Coating thickness for 4 application methods (5 replicates each)
set.seed(15)
df <- data.frame(
  thickness = c(rnorm(5, 125, 8), rnorm(5, 132, 8),
                rnorm(5, 119, 8), rnorm(5, 138, 8)),
  method    = factor(rep(c("Spray","Dip","Brush","Roll"), each = 5))
)

# Fit ANOVA
fit <- aov(thickness ~ method, data = df)

# ANOVA table
aov_tbl <- summary(fit)[[1]]
rownames(aov_tbl) <- trimws(rownames(aov_tbl))
print(aov_tbl)


          Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value  Pr(>F)  
method     3 577.96 192.654  4.1267 0.02403 *
Residuals 16 746.96  46.685                  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1




Code
# Extract values
MSE  <- aov_tbl["Residuals", "Mean Sq"]
df_E <- aov_tbl["Residuals", "Df"]
N    <- nrow(df); a <- nlevels(df$method); n <- N / a

cat("MSE =", round(MSE, 3), "\n")


MSE = 46.685 


Code
cat("σ̂  =", round(sqrt(MSE), 3), "\n")


σ̂  = 6.833 


Code
cat("F₀  =", round(aov_tbl["method", "F value"], 3), "\n")


F₀  = 4.127 


Code
cat("p   =", round(aov_tbl["method", "Pr(>F)"], 4), "\n\n")


p   = 0.024 


Code
# Fisher LSD
LSD <- qt(0.975, df = df_E) * sqrt(MSE * 2/n)
cat("LSD =", round(LSD, 3), "\n\n")


LSD = 9.161 


Code
# Group means and pairwise differences
means <- tapply(df$thickness, df$method, mean)
cat("Group means:\n"); print(round(means, 2))


Group means:


 Brush    Dip   Roll  Spray 
120.40 129.84 135.31 130.02 





If you suspect a nuisance factor (e.g., different operators, batches), use RCBD:


Code
# RCBD model: response ~ treatment + block
# fit_rcbd <- aov(y ~ treatment + block, data = df_rcbd)
# Key: df_Error = (a-1)(b-1) instead of N-a
cat("RCBD df_Error = (a-1)(b-1)\n")


RCBD df_Error = (a-1)(b-1)


Code
cat("CRD df_Error  = N-a\n")


CRD df_Error  = N-a


Code
cat("Blocking removes SS_Block from SS_Error → smaller MS_E → larger F₀\n")


Blocking removes SS_Block from SS_Error → smaller MS_E → larger F₀






Is the F-test significant at α = 0.05? Which methods differ significantly by LSD?
What does MS_E estimate in this context?
If you suspected that different operators applied each method, what design would you switch to?

title: CRD and One-Way ANOVA
text:
Identify when a CRD is appropriate and when blocking is needed
Set up the one-way ANOVA model and construct the ANOVA table
Apply Fisher’s LSD for pairwise comparisons
Run both balanced and unbalanced CRD in R and extract key values

title: CRD and One-Way ANOVA
text: A CRD randomly assigns experimental units to treatment levels. Use CRD when experimental units are homogeneous. If there is a known nuisance variable (time, batch, operator), use RCBD instead — blocking removes that variation from SS_Error.

title: CRD and One-Way ANOVA
text:
Source

a−1

MS_Trt/MS_E


Error
SS_E

N−1





\[\hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{MS_E}, \quad \text{Fisher LSD} = t_{\alpha/2,\,N-a}\sqrt{MS_E\left(\tfrac{1}{n_i}+\tfrac{1}{n_j}\right)}\]

title: CRD and One-Way ANOVA
text:
Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, rio, tidyverse)

# Coating thickness for 4 application methods (5 replicates each)
set.seed(15)
df <- data.frame(
  thickness = c(rnorm(5, 125, 8), rnorm(5, 132, 8),
                rnorm(5, 119, 8), rnorm(5, 138, 8)),
  method    = factor(rep(c("Spray","Dip","Brush","Roll"), each = 5))
)

# Fit ANOVA
fit <- aov(thickness ~ method, data = df)

# ANOVA table
aov_tbl <- summary(fit)[[1]]
rownames(aov_tbl) <- trimws(rownames(aov_tbl))
print(aov_tbl)


          Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value  Pr(>F)  
method     3 577.96 192.654  4.1267 0.02403 *
Residuals 16 746.96  46.685                  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1




Code
# Extract values
MSE  <- aov_tbl["Residuals", "Mean Sq"]
df_E <- aov_tbl["Residuals", "Df"]
N    <- nrow(df); a <- nlevels(df$method); n <- N / a

cat("MSE =", round(MSE, 3), "\n")


MSE = 46.685 


Code
cat("σ̂  =", round(sqrt(MSE), 3), "\n")


σ̂  = 6.833 


Code

F₀  = 4.127 


Code

p   = 0.024 


Code
# Fisher LSD
LSD <- qt(0.975, df = df_E) * sqrt(MSE * 2/n)
cat("LSD =", round(LSD, 3), "\n\n")


LSD = 9.161 


Code
# Group means and pairwise differences

cat("Group means:\n"); print(round(means, 2))

title: 2^k Factorial Designs
text:
Set up a 2^k design with ±1 coding and compute main effects and interactions
Distinguish replicated (use aov()) from unreplicated (use lm() + Lenth’s method) designs
Apply Effect = 2 × lm coefficient — avoid the common ×2 trap
Interpret a Daniel (normal probability) plot to screen significant effects




A 2^k design runs each of k factors at two levels (−1 = low, +1 = high). With k factors there are 2^k runs per replicate. The design is efficient: it estimates k main effects and all 2^(k−1) − 1 interactions with the minimum number of runs.
Key relationship: Effect = 2 × (lm coefficient). The lm() spans ±1 (range = 2), so the coefficient = Effect/2.



For single-replicate 2^k (no error df):
\[s_0 = 1.5 \cdot \text{median}(|e_j|), \quad PSE = 1.5 \cdot \text{median}(|e_j| < 2.5s_0)\]
\[ME = t_{\alpha/2,\; m/3} \cdot PSE\]
Effects with |effect| ≥ ME are considered significant.





Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, FrF2, tidyverse)

# 2^2 design with n=3 replicates: factors A (temperature) and B (pressure)
set.seed(7)
df <- expand.grid(A = c(-1, 1), B = c(-1, 1)) |>
  slice(rep(1:n(), each = 3)) |>
  mutate(y = 40 + 5*A + 3*B + 2*A*B + rnorm(12, 0, 2))

df$A <- factor(df$A); df$B <- factor(df$B)

fit_aov <- aov(y ~ A * B, data = df)
aov_tbl <- summary(fit_aov)[[1]]
rownames(aov_tbl) <- trimws(rownames(aov_tbl))
print(aov_tbl)


          Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
A          1 336.09  336.09  62.509 4.753e-05 ***
B          1 225.01  225.01  41.850 0.0001943 ***
A:B        1 122.96  122.96  22.869 0.0013868 ** 
Residuals  8  43.01    5.38                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1




Code
# Effect estimates via lm (numeric coding)
df_num <- df |> mutate(A = as.numeric(as.character(A)),
                        B = as.numeric(as.character(B)),
                        AB = A * B)
fit_lm <- lm(y ~ A + B + AB, data = df_num)

coefs <- coef(fit_lm)
cat("Effects (= 2 × coefficient):\n")


Effects (= 2 × coefficient):


Code
cat("  A  =", round(2 * coefs["A"], 3), "\n")


  A  = 10.584 


Code
cat("  B  =", round(2 * coefs["B"], 3), "\n")


  B  = 8.661 


Code
cat("  AB =", round(2 * coefs["AB"], 3), "\n")


  AB = 6.402 







Code
# 2^3 single replicate
design <- FrF2(8, 3, randomize = FALSE)
y <- c(22, 32, 35, 55, 44, 40, 60, 39)
design$y <- y

DanielPlot(lm(y ~ A * B * C, data = design),
           main = "Daniel Plot — Unreplicated 2^3",
           half = FALSE)





Daniel (normal probability) plot — points off the line are significant








Using aov() on a single-replicate design (0 error df — will fail or give nonsense)
Reporting lm() coefficients as effects (off by factor of 2)
Forgetting trimws() when extracting from summary(aov)[[1]]
Ignoring a borderline F: always compare to the critical value, not just eyeball the p-value





In the replicated 2^2: are both main effects significant? Is the interaction?
Change the true effect sizes in the simulation and observe how F₀ and the Daniel plot change.
What would you do if you ran a 2^4 with a single replicate and got borderline effects?





2^k Factorial Design
R: Factorial ANOVA
2^k Factorial Examples

title: Sampling Distributions and the CLT
text:
Explain the difference between a population distribution and a sampling distribution
State the Central Limit Theorem and identify when it applies
Recognize the normal, t, χ², and F distributions and when each is used
Compute normal and t probabilities in R




A sampling distribution is the distribution of a statistic (like x̄) across all possible samples of size n. You never see it directly — you only see one sample — but understanding it is the foundation of all inference.
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) says: for large enough n, x̄ is approximately normal regardless of the population distribution.
\[\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) \quad \text{(approximately, for large n)}\]
This is why we can make probability statements about sample means.






Distribution
Used for
df




Normal Z
Mean when σ known, or n ≥ 40
—


Student’s t
Mean when σ unknown, n small
n−1


Chi-square χ²
Variance, goodness-of-fit
varies


F
Ratio of variances, ANOVA, regression
(df₁, df₂)








Code
set.seed(123)

# Skewed population (exponential)
pop <- rexp(100000, rate = 1/10)  # mean = 10

# Draw 5000 samples of size n=30, compute each x̄
n_sim  <- 5000
n_samp <- 30
xbars  <- replicate(n_sim, mean(sample(pop, n_samp)))

# Plot
hist(xbars, breaks = 50, col = "steelblue", border = "white",
     main = paste("Sampling dist of x̄ (n =", n_samp, ")"),
     xlab = "Sample mean", probability = TRUE)
curve(dnorm(x, mean = mean(pop), sd = sd(pop)/sqrt(n_samp)),
      add = TRUE, col = "firebrick", lwd = 2)





Sampling distribution of x̄ from a skewed population, n=30









Code
# P(Z < 1.96)
pnorm(1.96)


[1] 0.9750021


Code
# P(Z > 1.96)  [upper tail]
pnorm(1.96, lower.tail = FALSE)


[1] 0.0249979


Code
# t critical value: df = 15, α/2 = 0.025
qt(0.975, df = 15)


[1] 2.13145


Code
# P(T₁₅ < 2.131)
pt(2.131, df = 15)


[1] 0.9749787






Change n_samp from 30 to 5. Does the sampling distribution still look normal?
Change rexp to runif(100000, 0, 20) (uniform population). Does CLT still hold?
What sample size is “large enough” for the CLT to kick in for an exponential population?





Distributions
Probability
Standard Error

title: Hypothesis Testing — The 7-Step Framework
text:
Apply the 7-step hypothesis testing procedure to any test
Distinguish Type I and Type II errors and explain their consequences
Interpret p-values using the teacher’s expected phrasing
Compute one-sample Z and t tests in R




Hypothesis testing is a formal decision procedure. You assume nothing is happening (H₀), then ask: “How unlikely is my data if H₀ were true?” If very unlikely (p-value < α), you reject H₀.
You never prove H₀ is true — you only fail to find evidence against it.










Step
Action




1
Identify the parameter of interest (μ, σ², p)


2
State H₀ (null hypothesis — “no effect”)


3
State H₁ (alternative — two-sided ≠, upper >, lower <)


4
Choose the test statistic formula


5
State the rejection criterion (critical value or p-value < α)


6
Compute the test statistic and p-value


7
Conclude in plain language, in context










H₀ True
H₀ False




Fail to reject H₀
Correct ✓
Type II error (β)


Reject H₀
Type I error (α)
Correct ✓ (Power = 1−β)






Reject H₀: > “Because p-value = [value] < α = [value], we reject H₀. There is sufficient evidence to conclude that [H₁ in context] at α = [value].”
Fail to reject H₀: > “Because p-value = [value] > α = [value], we fail to reject H₀. There is insufficient evidence to conclude that [H₁ in context] at α = [value].”




Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.



Run the interactive block above and verify these results:

One-sided test: Change alternative to "greater". How does the p-value change? Why does a one-sided test have more power?
Null shift: Change mu0 to 10.1. Does the conclusion change? How close to the sample mean does the null have to be before you fail to reject?
Significance level: Change alpha to 0.01. Does the same evidence lead to the same decision? What Type I error consequence does that tighter threshold represent in this context?





Hypothesis Testing Overview
Which Test?
R: Hypothesis Tests
Power and Sample Size

title: Control Chart Concepts
text:
Explain the purpose of a control chart and the difference between common and special cause variation
Choose the correct chart type for a given situation
Apply the Western Electric run rules to detect out-of-control signals
Distinguish control limits from specification limits




A control chart monitors a process over time to detect special cause variation (unexpected shifts, trends, or spikes) against the background of common cause variation (natural process noise).
Control limits are set at ±3σ from the process center — not at specification limits. A point outside control limits signals a process change worth investigating.






Data type
Subgroup size
Chart




Measurements (continuous)
n ≥ 2
X̄/R (n ≤ 10) or X̄/S (n > 10)


Fraction defective
Variable
p-chart


Count of defects (fixed area)
Fixed
c-chart


Defects per unit (variable area)
Variable
u-chart






X̄/R chart:
\[UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R}, \quad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}\]
\[UCL_R = D_4\bar{R}, \quad LCL_R = D_3\bar{R}\]
where A₂, D₃, D₄ are control chart constants from the table (depend on n).
p-chart:
\[UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}, \quad LCL_p = \max\!\left(0,\; \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}\right)\]



A process is out of control if any of the following occur:

One point beyond ±3σ
Two of three consecutive points beyond ±2σ (same side)
Four of five consecutive points beyond ±1σ (same side)
Eight consecutive points on the same side of the center line








Control Limits
Specification Limits




Set by
Process data (±3σ)
Engineering / customer


Purpose
Detect process changes
Define acceptable product


Relationship
A capable process has CL inside spec limits





A process can be in control but not capable if its natural variation exceeds the spec limits.

Process Capability

title: X̄/R and X̄/S Charts
text:
Build X̄/R charts in R using the qcc package (R chart first, always)
Identify out-of-control points and revise limits
Compute Cp and Cpk and interpret process capability
State conclusions using the teacher’s expected phrasing





Build R chart first — estimate σ̂ from R̄/d₂
Build X̄ chart using σ̂ from the R chart
Identify any out-of-control points
Remove special-cause points and revise limits
Compute Cp and Cpk against specification limits
State process capability conclusion






Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, qcc, tidyverse)

# Piston ring diameter data (n=5 per subgroup, 25 subgroups)
# Using built-in pistonrings dataset
data("pistonrings")

diameter <- qcc.groups(pistonrings$diameter, pistonrings$sample)

# Step 1: R chart
r_chart <- qcc(diameter, type = "R", nsigmas = 3,
               title = "R Chart — Piston Ring Diameter")











Code
# Step 2: X̄ chart (σ̂ from R chart)
xbar_chart <- qcc(diameter, type = "xbar", nsigmas = 3,
                  std.dev = sd.R(diameter),
                  title = "X̄ Chart — Piston Ring Diameter")









Code
cat("\nCenter (x̄̄):", round(xbar_chart$center, 4), "\n")



Center (x̄̄): 74.0036 


Code
cat("UCL:", round(xbar_chart$limits[2], 4), "\n")


UCL: 74.0171 


Code
cat("LCL:", round(xbar_chart$limits[1], 4), "\n")


LCL: 73.9901 


Code
cat("σ̂ :", round(xbar_chart$std.dev, 6), "\n")


σ̂ : 0.010071 




Code
# Process capability (LSL = 73.95, USL = 74.05 mm)
pc <- process.capability(xbar_chart, spec.limits = c(73.95, 74.05))










Process Capability Analysis

Call:
process.capability(object = xbar_chart, spec.limits = c(73.95,     74.05))

Number of obs = 200          Target = 74
       Center = 74              LSL = 73.95
       StdDev = 0.01007         USL = 74.05

Capability indices:

      Value   2.5%  97.5%
Cp    1.655  1.492  1.817
Cp_l  1.774  1.623  1.926
Cp_u  1.536  1.403  1.668
Cp_k  1.536  1.378  1.693
Cpm   1.558  1.397  1.719

Exp<LSL 0%   Obs<LSL 0%
Exp>USL 0%   Obs>USL 0%


Code
print(pc, digits = 6)

[1] 0.0100709

[1] 74

73.95 74.05

Cp   1.65493 1.49237 1.81728
Cp_l 1.77425 1.62291 1.92558

Cp_k 1.53561 1.37783 1.69339
Cpm  1.55811 1.39702 1.71899

$exp
Exp < LSL Exp > USL 
        0         0 

$obs
Obs < LSL Obs > USL 
        0         0 












Finding
What to say




All points in limits
“The process is in statistical control.”


Points outside limits
“Point(s) [#] fall outside the control limits, indicating the process is out of control.”


Cp ≥ 1.33, Cpk ≥ 1.33
“The process is capable of meeting specifications.”


Cp ≥ 1.33, Cpk < 1.33
“The process spread is adequate but the process is off-center.”


Cp < 1.33
“The process is not capable of meeting specifications.”

title: Multiple Regression
text:
Fit an MLR model and interpret each βⱼ coefficient
Use adjusted R² to compare models with different numbers of predictors
Test individual coefficients (t-test) and the overall model (F-test)
Add interaction terms and interpret them




MLR extends SLR to multiple predictors:
\[y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1} + \beta_2 x_{i2} + \cdots + \beta_k x_{ik} + \epsilon_i\]
βⱼ interpretation: holding all other predictors constant, a one-unit increase in xⱼ is associated with a βⱼ change in the mean response.
\[R^2_{adj} = 1 - \frac{SS_E/(n-p)}{SS_T/(n-1)}, \quad p = k+1\]
Adjusted R² penalizes for adding uninformative predictors; R² alone always increases.





Code
pacman::p_load(pacman, rio, tidyverse)

# Wire bond strength: y = pull strength, x1 = wire length, x2 = die height
# (Montgomery Table 12.1 — enter manually)
df <- tribble(
  ~strength, ~length, ~height,
  9.95,  2, 50,   24.45, 8, 110,  31.75, 11, 120,  35.00, 10, 550,
  25.02, 8, 295,  16.86,  4, 200,  14.38,  2, 375,  9.60,   2,  52,
  24.35, 9, 100,  27.50,  8, 300,  17.08,  4, 412, 37.00,  11, 400,
  41.95, 12, 500,  11.66,  2, 360,  21.65,  4, 205, 17.89,  4, 400,
  69.00, 20, 600,  10.30,  1, 585,  34.93, 10, 540,  46.59, 15, 250
)

fit_mlr <- lm(strength ~ length + height, data = df)
summary(fit_mlr)



Call:
lm(formula = strength ~ length + height, data = df)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-3.7857 -1.4659 -0.3836  1.2563  5.9610 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 2.122485   1.272092   1.669  0.11353    
length      2.751591   0.116739  23.570 2.01e-14 ***
height      0.012489   0.003266   3.824  0.00136 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 2.462 on 17 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9755,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9726 
F-statistic: 338.8 on 2 and 17 DF,  p-value: 2.013e-14




Code
anova(fit_mlr)


Analysis of Variance Table

Response: strength
          Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
length     1 4018.0  4018.0 663.019 4.652e-15 ***
height     1   88.6    88.6  14.619   0.00136 ** 
Residuals 17  103.0     6.1                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


Code
cat("\nR²_adj =", round(summary(fit_mlr)$adj.r.squared, 4), "\n")



R²_adj = 0.9726 


Code
cat("σ̂     =", round(summary(fit_mlr)$sigma, 4), "\n")


σ̂     = 2.4617 


Code
# Overall F-test p-value
f_info <- summary(fit_mlr)$fstatistic
p_overall <- pf(f_info[1], f_info[2], f_info[3], lower.tail = FALSE)
cat("Overall F p-value =", format(p_overall, digits = 4), "\n")


Overall F p-value = 2.013e-14 







Code
# Add interaction x1*x2
df <- df |> mutate(lh = length * height)
fit_int <- lm(strength ~ length + height + lh, data = df)
summary(fit_int)



Call:
lm(formula = strength ~ length + height + lh, data = df)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-2.5435 -0.9259 -0.0074  0.3276  5.3104 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 6.3366229  1.6090974   3.938  0.00118 ** 
length      2.1137804  0.2113855  10.000 2.75e-08 ***
height      0.0013286  0.0042111   0.315  0.75646    
lh          0.0015555  0.0004639   3.353  0.00404 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 1.945 on 16 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9856,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9829 
F-statistic: 365.7 on 3 and 16 DF,  p-value: 6.041e-15


Code
cat("\nCompare adjusted R²:\n")



Compare adjusted R²:


Code
cat("  Without interaction:", round(summary(fit_mlr)$adj.r.squared, 4), "\n")


  Without interaction: 0.9726 


Code
cat("  With interaction:   ", round(summary(fit_int)$adj.r.squared, 4), "\n")


  With interaction:    0.9829 






Which predictor has the larger t-statistic? What does that mean?
Does adding the interaction term improve adjusted R²?
Predict pull strength for a wire with length = 6, height = 300.

1.1 In Plain English
When you have k factors and each one is tested at exactly 2 levels (low/−1 and high/+1), the complete design has 2^k runs per replicate. This makes it efficient for screening — quickly identifying which of many factors actually matter — and for estimating main effects and interactions with a minimum of runs.
The ±1 coding is not cosmetic: it creates orthogonal contrasts, meaning all effect estimates are independent of each other.

1.2 When To Use

k factors, each at exactly 2 levels (quantitative or qualitative — High/Low, On/Off)
Goal is screening (finding important factors) or initial characterization
When k ≥ 4, often run as a single replicate (no replication) to limit cost

1.3 Formula(s)

1.3.1 ±1 Coding
Low level → −1; High level → +1. The interaction column is the product of the main effect columns.
2^2 design sign table:



Treatment
A
B
AB

2^3 design has 8 runs (cube vertices); sign table has columns I, A, B, AB, C, AC, BC, ABC.


1.3.2 Effect Estimation
\[\text{Effect}_A = \frac{1}{n \cdot 2^{k-1}} \cdot \text{Contrast}_A = \bar{y}_{A+} - \bar{y}_{A-}\]
The effect is the difference in means between high and low levels of A, averaged over all combinations of other factors.


1.3.3 Contrast → SS
\[SS = \frac{(\text{Contrast})^2}{n \cdot 2^k}\]
Each main effect and interaction has df = 1.


1.3.4 Degrees of Freedom Summary



Source
df




Each effect (A, B, AB, …)
1


All 2^k − 1 effects combined
2^k − 1


SSTotal
n·2^k − 1


SSError (with replication)
2^k·(n−1)





1.3.5 Linear Model (Regression Approach)
\[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_{12} x_1 x_2 + \varepsilon\]
where x₁, x₂ ∈ {−1, +1}.
Critical relationship: \[\hat{\beta} = \frac{\text{Effect}}{2} = \frac{\bar{y}_+ - \bar{y}_-}{2}\]
Exam trap: The lm() coefficient from R = Effect/2 = β̂. If asked for the effect, double the R coefficient.
The book (Minitab definition) reports Coef = Effect/2 (same as R), but β₀ in Minitab differs from R’s intercept — don’t try to match β₀ between R and Minitab.


1.3.6 Two Analysis Approaches in R









Approach
R call
Output
Use when




Regression
lm(obs ~ A + B + AB)
Coefficients = Effect/2; t-tests per term
Asked for regression equation or coefficient estimates


ANOVA
aov(obs ~ A * B)
F-tests, SS, MS per source
Asked for ANOVA table



Both give the same SS; they’re two views of the same analysis.


1.3.7 Unreplicated Designs (n = 1, k ≥ 4)
When n=1 (single replicate), df_error = 0 — the F-test is undefined. The solution:

Sparsity of effects principle: Only a few effects are large; most high-order interactions are negligible
Pool high-order interactions as an estimate of error, OR
Daniel plot (normal probability plot of effects): Real effects fall off the straight line; noise effects cluster near the line

FrF2::DanielPlot(lmmodel) in R — effects far from the normal probability line are flagged as significant.

1.3.7.1 Lenth’s PSE and ME (Formal Threshold for Unreplicated 2^k)
When you need a numerical cutoff rather than just a graphical read:
\[s_0 = 1.5 \times \text{median}(|effects|)\] \[PSE = 1.5 \times \text{median}(|effects| \text{ where } |e| < 2.5 s_0)\] \[ME = t_{\alpha/2,\; m/3} \times PSE\]
where m = total number of effects (m = 2^k − 1). For a 2^4 design: m = 15, df = m/3 = 5, so ME = t_{0.025,5} × PSE ≈ 2.571 × PSE. An effect with |e| ≥ ME is flagged significant. FrF2::DanielPlot() automates this calculation.


1.3.7.2 Effect Extraction from lm() When Using Colon-Notation
When lm(y ~ A*B*C*D) is used, R names interactions as A:B, A:C, etc. (with surrounding whitespace). To extract effects reliably:
# Build named vectors to map colon-notation → short labels
effect_terms  <- c("A", "B", "C", "D", "A:B", "A:C", "B:D", ...)
effect_labels <- c("A", "B", "C", "D", "AB",  "AC",  "BD",  ...)

coef_tbl <- summary(fit_lm)$coefficients
effects  <- 2 * coef_tbl[effect_terms, "Estimate"]   # Effect = 2 × coefficient
names(effects) <- effect_labels
Alternatively, pre-compute interaction columns in the data frame using mutate() (AB = A*B) and use plain names in the lm() formula — avoids the colon-notation lookup entirely and is less error-prone.


1.3.7.3 R Pattern: Single Replicate (n = 1)
Do NOT use aov() when n=1 — df_error = 0 and F-tests are undefined. Use a saturated lm() instead:
# 2^5 with n=1: pre-compute all interaction columns, then fit saturated model
df <- df %>%
  mutate(AB=A*B, AC=A*C, AD=A*D, AE=A*E,
         BC=B*C, BD=B*D, BE=B*E,
         CD=C*D, CE=C*E, DE=D*E, ...)  # as needed

fit_lm <- lm(obs ~ A+B+C+D+E+AB+AC+AD+AE+BC+BD+BE+CD+CE+DE, data=df)
coef_tbl <- summary(fit_lm)$coefficients
effects  <- 2 * coef_tbl[effect_terms, "Estimate"]   # Effect = 2 × coef
Key nuclear pump results (2^5, n=1): A=75.25, B=−48.75, C=158.00, D=−19.63, E=−35.25, AC=57.75, CE=−61.75.


1.3.7.4 R Pattern: Two-Replicate (n = 2)
When data has R1/R2 columns, pivot to long format first, then use aov():
dft <- df %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(R1, R2), names_to = "Rep", values_to = "Response") %>%
  mutate_at(vars(-Response), as.factor)

fit <- aov(Response ~ A * B * C, data = dft)
aov_tbl <- summary(fit)[[1]]
rownames(aov_tbl) <- trimws(rownames(aov_tbl))   # MANDATORY — R pads names with spaces
Key cutting tool results (2^3, n=2): F_B=11.53 sig, F_C=8.36 sig, F_AC=23.10 sig; F_A=0.54 not sig.


1.3.7.5 Three-Replicate Pattern (pivot_longer with 3 columns)
Same as two-replicate but with cols = c(R1, R2, R3). The extra replicate improves power but the workflow is identical.


1.3.7.6 setwd Pattern for Drill Scripts
HWK scripts in Section_4/Drills/ reference CSV/xlsx files by relative path. Add this after p_load() to auto-set working directory when opened in RStudio:
if (rstudioapi::isAvailable()) {
  setwd(dirname(rstudioapi::getActiveDocumentContext()$path))
}
Without this, running from a project root causes “file not found” for data files.



1.3.8 R vs. Book (Minitab) Coefficients




R (lm() coefficient)
Book / Minitab Coef




Value
Effect / 2
Effect / 2


Same?
Yes — they match



β₀
R intercept ≠ Minitab constant
Not directly comparable



The R coefficients are the same as Minitab’s “Coef” column, but not Minitab’s “Effect” column (which is 2× larger). The ANOVA tables are identical.

1.4 Key Assumptions

±1 coding is applied correctly (pre-computed interaction columns in data)
Normality and constant variance (same as any ANOVA)
For unreplicated designs: sparsity of effects — only a few effects are real

1.5 Common Mistakes

Reading lm() coefficient as the effect — it is the effect divided by 2; if asked for the effect, multiply the R coefficient by 2
Trying to F-test an unreplicated design without pooling — df_error = 0 and the test fails; use lm() + DanielPlot or Lenth’s ME instead
Using the no-gather pattern: for 2^k designs with no replication, the data already has one row per treatment combination — do not gather() (comment in 6-2kFD 5Factor.R: ## no replications = no gather)
Confusing the ±1 coded AB interaction column (product of A and B columns) with a separate variable that needs manual coding — aov(obs ~ A * B) handles this automatically when A and B are already ±1 factors
Null result ≠ no real effect — high within-cell variance (large MS_Error) can make even a real biological or physical effect non-significant. Root vole experiment (2^2, n=3): within-cell SD ≈ 58 in no-predator cells vs ≈ 18 in predator cells → MS_Error = 1835, F_Pred = 5.08 non-significant at α = 0.05. A power-insufficient study can fail to detect real effects.
Borderline F requires exact comparison — never judge an F value qualitatively. F_Pred = 5.08 vs F_crit(1,8) = 5.318 → not significant. The 0.23-unit gap matters. Always compute or look up F_crit and compare directly.
Heteroscedasticity tied to a factor level — if one treatment-level has SD ≈ 3× the others, constant variance is violated. Root vole: max/min cell SD ratio ≈ 3.3×. Check residual vs fitted plot and note which cell/level is driving the inequality.

1.6 Related

factorial-anova — general factorial for any number of levels
factorial-anova-r — R code including DanielPlot() and the 4-model hierarchy
2k-factorial-examples — worked 2^2 and 2^3 examples with effect calculations
anova-design-guide — design selection guide
ss-decomposition — each 2^k effect gets one SS row in this framework
Code

1.1 In Plain English
A confidence interval gives a range of plausible values for an unknown population parameter. “95% confident” means: if we repeated this process many times, 95% of the intervals we build would contain the true parameter. It does NOT mean there is a 95% chance μ is in this particular interval.

1.2 Teacher’s Phrasing

“We are [100(1−α)]% confident that [parameter in context] is between [lower] and [upper].”

1.3 General Structure
\[\text{Estimate} \pm \text{Score} \times \text{Standard Error}\]

1.4 CI on Mean μ

1.4.1 Case 1: Variance Known (or n ≥ 40, use CLT) → Z interval
\[\bar{x} - z_{\alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu \leq \bar{x} + z_{\alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]
One-sided bounds: - Upper: \(\mu \leq \bar{x} + z_\alpha \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\) - Lower: \(\bar{x} - z_\alpha \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu\)
Sample size to achieve margin of error E: \[n = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma}{E}\right)^2\]


1.4.2 Case 2: Variance Unknown, Normal Population → t interval
\[\bar{x} - t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu \leq \bar{x} + t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\]

Use t-table with df = n − 1
As n → ∞, t → z

section: 1.5 CI on Variance σ² → χ² interval
text:
1.5 CI on Variance σ² → χ² interval
\[\frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}} \leq \sigma^2 \leq \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-1}}\]
CI on σ: take square root of both bounds.
One-sided bounds on σ²: - Lower: \(\sigma^2 \geq \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha,n-1}}\) - Upper: \(\sigma^2 \leq \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha,n-1}}\)

1.6 Common Z Critical Values

1.7 Z vs. t Decision

σ known → Z
σ unknown, normal population → t (df = n−1)
σ unknown, n ≥ 40 → Z (CLT approximation acceptable)

1.8 Key Assumptions

Random sample
Normal population (or n large for CLT)
Independence of observations

1.9 Common Mistakes

Using Z when σ is unknown and n is small
Forgetting to take square root for CI on σ (vs σ²)
Misidentifying α/2 vs α for one-sided intervals

1.10 Related

distributions
hypothesis-testing-overview
which-test
two-sample-tests
regression-ci — CI on βⱼ uses the same Estimate ± Score × SE structure
Code

1.1 Pearson Correlation Coefficient r
\[\hat{\rho} = r = \frac{S_{xy}}{\sqrt{S_{xx} \cdot S_{yy}}}\]



Symbol
Meaning




S_{xy}
Σ(xᵢ−x̄)(yᵢ−ȳ) = Σxᵢyᵢ − nx̄ȳ


S_{xx}
Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²


S_{yy}
Σ(yᵢ−ȳ)² = SST



Properties: - Range: −1 ≤ r ≤ 1 - r = +1: perfect positive linear relationship - r = −1: perfect negative linear relationship - r = 0: no linear relationship (may still have nonlinear relationship) - SLR only: R² = r²

1.2 Hypothesis Test: H₀: ρ = 0
\[T_0 = r\sqrt{\frac{n-2}{1-r^2}}, \quad T_0 \sim t(n-2)\]
This test is mathematically equivalent to the t-test on β₁ in SLR. If there is significant correlation, there is a significant linear relationship (and vice versa).
p-value: 2 * pt(-abs(T0), df = n-2)
No-data workflow: When r and n are given directly (no dataset), skip lm() entirely — set r and n as scalars and apply the formula above. Do not try to reconstruct raw data.
r  <- 0.83          # given sample correlation
n  <- 25            # given sample size
T0 <- r * sqrt((n - 2) / (1 - r^2))
p_val <- 2 * pt(-abs(T0), df = n - 2)

cor(df$x, df$y)            # sample correlation r from data
cor.test(df$x, df$y)       # full hypothesis test: t stat, p-value, 95% CI on ρ

section: 1.3 Fisher Z-Transform: H₀: ρ = ρ₀ (Non-Zero Null)
text:
1.3 Fisher Z-Transform: H₀: ρ = ρ₀ (Non-Zero Null)
Use when comparing correlation strength to a specific non-zero reference value (e.g., “is the correlation at least 0.5?”). The T₀ above is only valid for H₀: ρ = 0; for any other null, use the Fisher Z-transform.
Transform: \(Z = \text{arctanh}(r) = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1+r}{1-r}\right)\)
Test statistic (eq. 11.49/11.50): \[Z_0 = \left(\text{arctanh}(\hat{r}) - \text{arctanh}(\rho_0)\right)\sqrt{n-3} \sim N(0,1)\]
Compare Z₀ to z_{α/2} = 1.96 (for two-sided α = 0.05).
p-value: 2 * pnorm(-abs(Z0))

Rounding instruction: Some textbook problems specify “use z-values rounded to two decimal places.” This means round arctanh(r) and arctanh(ρ₀) to 2 decimal places before computing Z₀ or CI bounds — it changes numerical answers materially.

1.4 Confidence Interval on ρ (Fisher Z-Transform)
\[\text{CI on }\rho: \quad \tanh\!\left(\text{arctanh}(\hat{r}) \pm \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{\sqrt{n-3}}\right)\]
Steps: 1. Compute \(w = \text{arctanh}(r)\) 2. Form bounds: \(w \pm 1.96/\sqrt{n-3}\) 3. Back-transform: \(\rho_{L,U} = \tanh(\text{bound})\)
Note: This CI is on ρ itself, not on Z. The CI from cor.test() uses this same Fisher Z method.
r  <- 0.83
n  <- 25
rho0 <- 0.5       # non-zero null value

# Fisher Z transform
w    <- atanh(r)          # arctanh(r) — R function
w0   <- atanh(rho0)       # arctanh(ρ₀)

# Test statistic for H₀: ρ = ρ₀
Z0   <- (w - w0) * sqrt(n - 3)
p_val_fisher <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(Z0))

# 95% CI on ρ
margin <- 1.96 / sqrt(n - 3)
CI_low  <- tanh(w - margin)   # tanh = back-transform
CI_high <- tanh(w + margin)
Which p-value formula to use:



Null hypothesis
Test statistic
p-value formula




H₀: ρ = 0
T₀ ~ t(n−2)
2 * pt(-abs(T0), n-2)


H₀: ρ = ρ₀ (ρ₀ ≠ 0)
Z₀ ~ N(0,1)
2 * pnorm(-abs(Z0))

1.5 When to Transform
Transformations are needed when residual plots reveal violations of regression assumptions: - Non-linearity (curved residual pattern) → transform x or y - Non-constant variance (fan shape) → transform y
How to choose: Look at the scatter plot of y vs x and the residual plot. The shape of the relationship suggests what transformation works.

section: 1.6 Common Transformations and When to Use Them
text:
1.6 Common Transformations and When to Use Them



Original Relationship
Transform
Linearized Model




Y = β₀ eˢ¹ˣ (exponential)
Y* = ln(Y)
Y* = ln(β₀) + β₁x


Y = β₀ xᵝ¹ (power)
Y* = ln(Y), x* = ln(x)
Y* = ln(β₀) + β₁x*


Y = β₀ + β₁ ln(x) (log-linear)
x* = ln(x)
Y = β₀ + β₁x*


Y = β₀ + β₁/x (reciprocal)
x* = 1/x
Y = β₀ + β₁x*


Y = β₀ + β₁√x (square root)
x* = √x
Y = β₀ + β₁x*



Key caution: Back-transforming predictions: if you transformed Y → ln(Y), the prediction is ln(ŷ). To get ŷ in original units: exp(predicted value). Note this is an estimate of the median (not mean) of the original-scale distribution.

1.7 R Code for Transformations
df <- df %>% mutate(
  log_y  = log(y),      # natural log of y
  log_x  = log(x),      # natural log of x
  inv_x  = 1/x,         # reciprocal of x
  sqrt_x = sqrt(x),     # square root of x
  sq_x   = x^2          # polynomial term
)

# Fit transformed model:
fit_log <- lm(log_y ~ x, data = df)
summary(fit_log)

# Back-transform prediction:
x0 <- 30
pred_log <- predict(fit_log, newdata = data.frame(x = x0))
pred_original_scale <- exp(pred_log)

1.8 Choosing the Right Transformation
Step 1: Plot y vs x → what is the shape? - Concave up (increasing rate) → try ln(x) or √x on x - Exponential growth → try ln(y) on y - Power relationship → try ln(y) and ln(x) both
Step 2: Fit the transformed model and check residual plots - Are residuals now random? Constant spread? Normal Q-Q good? - If yes → transformation worked
Step 3: Verify R² improved and residual pattern resolved

1.9 Teacher’s Phrasing for Correlation
Significant correlation: “Because p-value = [X] < α = [X], we reject H₀: ρ = 0. There is sufficient evidence of a significant linear relationship between [X] and [Y].”
Non-significant: “Because p-value = [X] > α = [X], we fail to reject H₀: ρ = 0. There is insufficient evidence of a significant linear relationship.”

1.10 Key Assumptions

Pearson r measures only linear association — two variables can be strongly related but have r ≈ 0 if the relationship is nonlinear
Both variables should be continuous

1.11 Common Mistakes

Concluding no relationship when r ≈ 0 (only means no linear relationship)
Forgetting to back-transform predictions from a log model
Comparing r² between a transformed and untransformed model (not valid comparison)

1.12 Related

regression-slr
model-adequacy
regression-ht

Degrees of freedom (df) = number of values free to vary after constraints are applied. Each estimated parameter “uses up” one df. df determines which t, F, or χ² distribution to use for critical values and p-values. Getting df wrong = wrong critical value = wrong conclusion.

section: 1.3 How to Find the Right t, F, or χ² Critical Value in R
text:
1.3 How to Find the Right t, F, or χ² Critical Value in R
# t critical value (two-sided, α = 0.05, df = 18)
qt(0.975, df = 18)          # upper tail area = α/2

# t critical value (one-sided upper, α = 0.05, df = 18)
qt(0.95, df = 18)

# χ² critical value (upper tail)
qchisq(0.95, df = 10)

# F critical value (upper tail)
qf(0.95, df1 = 3, df2 = 20)
Logic: q*(p, df) returns the quantile at cumulative probability p. For two-sided α = 0.05, use p = 1 − α/2 = 0.975. For upper-tail only, use p = 1 − α = 0.95.

1.4 Common Mistakes







Mistake
Correct




Using df = n for one-sample t
df = n − 1


Using df = n−2 for MLR t-tests
df = n − k − 1


Forgetting df₁ and df₂ are separate for F
F always has two df values


Using n₁+n₂−1 for pooled t
pooled t df = n₁ + n₂ − 2


Using n (# pairs) as df for paired t
df = n − 1 for paired t

1.1 In Plain English
A factorial experiment tests all possible combinations of two or more factors simultaneously. This lets you detect not just whether each factor matters individually (main effects), but also whether the effect of one factor depends on the level of another (interaction effects). Running factors one-at-a-time misses interactions entirely.
The key insight: if an interaction is present, interpreting main effects alone gives the wrong answer.

1.2 When To Use

Two or more factors of interest (not nuisance — those go in RCBD)
You want to investigate main effects and interaction effects
You have n ≥ 2 replicates per cell (required to estimate error)

1.3 Formula(s)

1.3.1 2-Factor Model (eq 14.1)
\[Y_{ijk} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + (\tau\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}\] \[i=1,\ldots,a \quad j=1,\ldots,b \quad k=1,\ldots,n\]

τ_i = main effect of A; β_j = main effect of B; (τβ)_ij = interaction effect



1.3.2 2-Factor Hypotheses



Test
H₀
H₁




Factor A main effect
τ₁ = … = τ_a = 0
at least one τ_i ≠ 0


Factor B main effect
β₁ = … = β_b = 0
at least one β_j ≠ 0


AB Interaction
(τβ)₁₁ = … = (τβ)_ab = 0
at least one (τβ)_ij ≠ 0

1.3.7 R Formula Shorthand







R model formula

The * operator is required for factorial designs.

1.4 Diagnostic Rules for Constant Variance
3× rule: If one cell’s within-cell standard deviation is approximately 3× larger than the other cells’ SDs, the constant variance assumption is not reasonable.
Example: cell (Solder=260°C, Method=1) had within-cell SD ≈ 0.40 μm while all other cells ranged 0.07–0.13 μm. SD ratio ≈ 3.1× → constant variance NOT reasonable. Flag this in the residual vs fitted plot as a fan or funnel shape localized to that factor-level combination.
Borderline cases: When max/min cell SD ratio is between 2× and 3×, use judgment and state which cell is driving the inequality.

1.5 Marginality Principle (Hierarchical Models)
If the AB interaction is retained in the model, both A and B must remain even if B’s main-effect p-value is large. Dropping B from a model that contains AB creates an uninterpretable model because B’s effect is inseparable from the interaction.
Teacher’s phrasing: “Factor B is retained for model hierarchy (the AB interaction is present).”

section: 1.6 Non-Significant Interaction — Implication for Factor Recommendations
text:
1.6 Non-Significant Interaction — Implication for Factor Recommendations
If the interaction F-test is not significant, the optimal level of Factor A does not change depending on Factor B’s level. The factors act independently, and main effect conclusions apply across all levels of the other factor.
This is often tested directly: “If there is no significant interaction, does the recommended level of A change as B changes?” Answer: No.

1.7 LSD on Factorial Marginal Means
When the interaction is negligible, apply Fisher’s LSD to the marginal means of the significant factor. Use the full model’s df_E and the marginal-mean sample size:
\[LSD = t_{\alpha/2,\, df_E} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot MS_E}{n_{\text{marginal}}}}\]
where df_E = ab(n−1) and n_marginal = a·n (the number of observations in each marginal mean for factor B, averaging over all levels of A).
In R: agricolae::LSD.test(fit, "FactorB", group = TRUE)
Key warping results (a=4 Cu%, b=4 Temp, n=3): MS_E from aov(); LSD = 2.76; significant pairs: 40v60, 40v80, 40v100, 60v100, 80v100; NOT 60v80.

1.8 Key Assumptions

Normality of residuals — check Normal Q-Q plot
Constant variance across all cells — check Residuals vs Fitted
Independence — runs are randomized
n ≥ 2 replicates per cell — required to estimate error df = ab(n−1) > 0

1.9 Common Mistakes

Using F1 + F2 instead of F1 * F2 in aov() — the + model has zero interaction df and estimates no interaction
Interpreting main effects when the interaction F-test is significant — the interaction supersedes main effects
Confusing df_error = ab(n−1) with df_error = (a−1)(b−1); the latter is RCBD (no replication within cells)

1.10 Related

1.1 In Plain English
Use when you want to test whether observed category counts fit a claimed probability distribution. You compare what you observed (Oᵢ) to what you would expect under H₀ (Eᵢ = n·pᵢ). Large discrepancies produce a large χ² statistic. The test is always upper-tail only — a large χ² means poor fit.

1.2 When To Use

Testing whether data follows a specified distribution (uniform, binomial, Poisson, normal, etc.)
Data are categorical counts across k categories
All expected counts Eᵢ ≥ 5 (combine categories if needed)

1.3 Formula(s)



Symbol
Meaning




Oᵢ
observed frequency in category i


Eᵢ
expected frequency = n·pᵢ


k
number of categories


p
number of distribution parameters estimated from the data



Test statistic: \[\chi_0^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}\]
Degrees of freedom — GENERAL FORM: \[df = k - 1 - p\]
where p = number of parameters estimated from the sample data.








Situation
p
df




Probabilities fully specified (e.g., uniform, or given p₁…pₖ)
0
k − 1


Testing normal distribution, estimating μ and σ from data
2
k − 3


Testing Poisson, estimating λ from data
1
k − 2


Testing binomial, estimating p from data
1
k − 2




Note on the quick reference: The Exam 2 quick reference sheet listed df = k−1. This is the special case when p = 0 (expected probabilities are fully specified in the problem). The general formula used in class is df = k − 1 − p. If you estimate any parameters from the data, subtract that count from df.

Rejection criterion: Reject H₀ if χ₀² > χ²_{α, df} (always upper-tail)
Computing Eᵢ: \[E_i = n \cdot p_i\] where pᵢ is the probability assigned to category i under H₀.

1.4 Key Assumptions

Observations are independent
Each expected frequency Eᵢ ≥ 5 (if not, merge adjacent categories and recount k)
Categories are mutually exclusive and exhaustive

1.5 Common Mistakes

Using df = k−1 when parameters were estimated from data — subtract p from df
Forgetting that Eᵢ = n·pᵢ (not the same as the observed count)
Using this test when data are continuous — bin into categories first, or use KS test
Applying a two-tail test — GoF is always upper-tail only

1.6 Related

ht-one-sample-chisq-variance — different χ² test, on σ²
chi-square — broader coverage of all χ² procedures

1.1 In Plain English
Use when you want to test a claim about the population variance σ². The test statistic is formed by scaling the sample variance S² by the hypothesized variance σ₀² — if H₀ is true, this ratio follows a chi-square distribution. Unlike Z and t tests, the χ² distribution is right-skewed, so the critical regions are asymmetric.

1.2 When To Use

Testing H₀: σ² = σ₀²
One sample from a normal population
Want to verify a process has acceptable variability

1.3 Formula(s)



Symbol
Meaning




S²
sample variance


σ₀²
hypothesized variance (from H₀)


n
sample size


df
n − 1



Test statistic: \[\chi_0^2 = \frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma_0^2} \sim \chi^2_{n-1}\]
Hypothesis table:



H₁
Reject H₀ if




σ² ≠ σ₀²
χ₀² > χ²_{α/2, n−1} or χ₀² < χ²_{1−α/2, n−1}


σ² > σ₀²
χ₀² > χ²_{α, n−1}


σ² < σ₀²
χ₀² < χ²_{1−α, n−1}



Confidence interval on σ²: \[\frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}} \leq \sigma^2 \leq \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-1}}\]
Note: the lower bound uses the larger χ² critical value (right tail), upper bound uses the smaller (left tail).

1.4 Key Assumptions

Population must be normal (χ² test on variance is not robust to non-normality)
Observations are independent

1.5 Common Mistakes

Swapping χ²_{α/2} and χ²_{1−α/2} when computing CI bounds — remember larger χ² → smaller bound
Using this test when the population is clearly non-normal (use a different approach)
Confusing this χ² test (on σ²) with the GoF χ² test (on distribution fit)

1.6 Related

ht-independence — different χ² test, for contingency tables

1.1 In Plain English
Use when you know the population variance and want to test a claim about the population mean. Because σ is known, the test statistic follows the standard normal distribution exactly. Also valid when n ≥ 40 and σ is unknown, because the CLT makes the sampling distribution approximately normal and S ≈ σ.

1.2 When To Use

Testing H₀: μ = μ₀
σ² is known (given in the problem) OR n ≥ 40 (CLT applies — use S in place of σ)
Population is normal (or n large enough for CLT)

1.3 Formula(s)



Symbol
Meaning

σ

Test statistic: \[Z_0 = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0,1)\]

Type II error (two-sided test): \[\beta = \Phi\!\left(z_{\alpha/2} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right)\]

Sample size (one-sided): \(n \approx \dfrac{(z_\alpha+z_\beta)^2\sigma^2}{\delta^2}\)

1.4 Key Assumptions

Observations are independent
Population is normal OR n ≥ 40 (CLT)
σ² is truly known (not estimated from this sample)

1.5 Common Mistakes

Using Z when σ is unknown and n < 40 — use ht-one-sample-t-mean instead
Forgetting to halve α for two-sided tests when looking up z critical value
Confusing “n ≥ 40” as a hard rule — it is a guideline; normality matters for small n

1.1 In Plain English
Use when observations come in matched pairs — the same subject measured twice (before/after), two methods applied to the same unit, or subjects matched by a nuisance variable. By working with differences Dᵢ = X₁ᵢ − X₂ᵢ, you eliminate between-subject variability, making the test more powerful than an independent two-sample T-test.

1.2 When To Use

Two measurements on the same or matched subjects
H₀: μ_D = Δ₀ (usually Δ₀ = 0, i.e., no difference)
Differences D are approximately normally distributed

1.3 Formula(s)



Symbol
Meaning




Dᵢ
difference for pair i: X₁ᵢ − X₂ᵢ


D̄
sample mean of differences


Sᴅ
sample standard deviation of differences


n
number of pairs


Δ₀
hypothesized mean difference (usually 0)



Step 1 — Compute differences: Dᵢ = X₁ᵢ − X₂ᵢ for each pair
Step 2 — Compute D̄ and Sᴅ from the n differences
Test statistic: \[T_0 = \frac{\bar{D} - \Delta_0}{S_D / \sqrt{n}} \sim t_{n-1}\]
df: n − 1 (number of pairs minus 1)
Hypothesis table:



H₁
p-value
Reject H₀ if




μ_D ≠ Δ₀
prob in both tails
|T₀| > t_{α/2, n−1}


μ_D > Δ₀
prob above T₀
T₀ > t_{α, n−1}


μ_D < Δ₀
prob below T₀
T₀ < −t_{α, n−1}



Confidence interval on μ_D: \[\bar{d} - t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\frac{S_D}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu_D \leq \bar{d} + t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\frac{S_D}{\sqrt{n}}\]

1.4 Key Assumptions

Pairs are independent of each other
Differences Dᵢ are approximately normally distributed (robust for large n)
The pairing is meaningful — each X₁ᵢ is genuinely linked to its X₂ᵢ

1.5 Common Mistakes

Using independent two-sample T on paired data — loses power, inflates error variance
Computing the standard error using individual observations instead of differences
Using n₁ + n₂ − 2 degrees of freedom — df is n − 1 (n = number of pairs)
Forgetting to define D direction consistently (X₁ − X₂ vs X₂ − X₁ changes the sign of T₀)

1.6 Related

ht-two-sample-t-means — use when observations are independent
hypothesis-tests-r — t.test(x, y, paired = TRUE)
